brief: GAP/WHY: Leadless pacemakers (LPMs) avoid transvenous leads and subcutaneous pockets, lowering lead/pocket-related complications; whether neighborhood income affects in-hospital outcomes after LPM implantation is poorly characterized. AIM: assess association between ZIP-code-based median household income and in-hospital outcomes after LPM implantation in US. DESIGN/SETTING: retrospective observational analysis of National Inpatient Sample (NIS), 2018-2022; discharge weights applied to estimate national totals. POPULATION: adult LPM implantations; 41,985 weighted admissions (8397 unweighted). COMPARISON: stratified by income quartile Q1 (lowest) to Q4 (highest); multivariable logistic regression giving adjusted odds ratios (aORs) for in-hospital mortality, pericardial complications, bleeding, transfusion, venous thromboembolism (VTE), renal complications, device removal/revision; adjusted for demographics, comorbidities, payer, hospital region. BASELINE: Q1 younger (median 77.0 vs 81.0 yr; p<0.001), more diabetes (44.9% vs 33.8%), more end-stage renal disease (43.4% vs 36.6%). RESULTS: crude mortality highest in Q1 (4.7%); after adjustment Q2 had higher odds of in-hospital death vs Q1 (aOR 1.517; 95% CI 1.115-2.064; p=0.008). Pericardial complications rose with income (Q1 3.2% vs Q4 4.1%); Q4 higher adjusted odds vs Q1 (aOR 1.753; 95% CI 1.244-2.469; p=0.001). Q4 lower adjusted odds of bleeding (aOR 0.788; 95% CI 0.633-0.982; p=0.034) and transfusion (aOR 0.645; 95% CI 0.494-0.842; p=0.001). VTE more likely in Q2 and Q4 vs Q1. Median hospital charges (billed) highest in Q4; median length of stay longer in Q1 (6.0 vs 5.0 days; p<0.001). TAKEAWAY: neighborhood income has endpoint-specific (not uniform) associations with in-hospital LPM outcomes; patterns may inform peri-procedural counseling and institutional review.
